county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: North Slope Borough organized 1972, the largest borough by area in the U.S. Covers Alaska's entire Arctic coast from Point Hope to Kaktovik. Home to Inupiaq peoples with thousands of years of continuous habitation. Prudhoe Bay oil discovery (1968) and Trans-Alaska Pipeline transformed the borough's economy. Borough seat is Utqiagvik (formerly Barrow), the northernmost city in the U.S.
county_metadata.research_tips: North Slope Borough covers Alaska's Arctic coast, home to Inupiaq peoples. The borough government in Utqiagvik (formerly Barrow) maintains modern administrative records. Ilagiit Heritage Foundation in Utqiagvik documents Inupiaq family connections and oral histories. Presbyterian mission records from Barrow (1890s) are at the Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia. Episcopal mission records for Point Hope and other communities at the Diocese of Alaska archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Second Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for Arctic communities. Federal census from 1880 for the Arctic coast. Whaling records (1850s-1910s commercial whaling era) at the New Bedford Whaling Museum and NARA document contact-era families. The Arctic Slope Regional Corporation (ANCSA) in Utqiagvik has shareholder genealogical records. UAF holds oral histories and the Waldo Bodfish collection. The North Slope Borough Inupiaq History, Language and Culture Commission documents family histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for North Slope area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Archives, Juneau / Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for North Slope area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Probate and Court Records -- Second Judicial District Superior Court
source_profiles.repository: Alaska Superior Court, Second Judicial District / Alaska State Archives, Juneau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court handles probate (wills, estates, guardianships), adoption, and civil cases for North Slope. Second Judicial District records. Older territorial court files transferred to Alaska State Archives. Commissioner's court records from the territorial period may predate borough organization

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for North Slope area from 1880 to 1950; Alaska was first enumerated in the 1880 federal census. Coverage for remote villages is often incomplete or delayed in early decades. Alaska was a district/territory until statehood in 1959
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage varies; remote communities may not have been enumerated until 1920 or later

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Church and Mission Records
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / Denominational archives / Alaska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Presbyterian and Friends (Quaker) mission records from the 1890s for Arctic and Northwest Alaska communities. Presbyterian Historical Society in Philadelphia and Friends Church archives. Episcopal mission records at the Diocese of Alaska. Church records document families before territorial vital registration in 1913

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Bureau of Indian Affairs Records -- NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Alaska Native communities in North Slope area including census rolls, school enrollment records, allotment files, and agency correspondence. Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Land Patents, Homesteads, and Mining Claims -- BLM GLO / Alaska DNR
source_profiles.repository: BLM General Land Office Records (online) / Alaska DNR Recorder's Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents and homestead entries for North Slope area searchable on BLM GLO Records website. Alaska DNR Recorder's Office holds recorded documents (deeds, mortgages) for the recording district covering North Slope. Mining claims (both lode and placer) at BLM Alaska State Office. Alaska was a public land state -- most land was federal domain

source_profiles.source_name: North Slope Military Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA St. Louis / NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military installation records for North Slope area at NARA St. Louis and NARA Pacific Alaska Region

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Northwest Arctic Borough organized 1986. Covers the Kobuk River valley, Noatak River region, and Kotzebue Sound. Home to Inupiaq peoples. Kotzebue is the regional hub and borough seat. NANA Regional Corporation (ANCSA) is a major economic entity. Red Dog Mine is the world's largest zinc mine. Quaker missionaries arrived in the 1890s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Northwest Arctic Borough covers the Kobuk and Noatak River regions and Kotzebue, home to Inupiaq peoples. Quaker (Friends) mission records from the 1890s for Kotzebue and surrounding villages are at the Friends Church Alaska archives and Swarthmore College. Episcopal mission records for Kobuk River communities at the Diocese of Alaska archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Second Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for Northwest Arctic communities. Federal census from 1880 for the Kotzebue Sound area, though coverage of remote villages is limited before 1920. The NANA Regional Corporation (ANCSA) in Kotzebue has shareholder genealogical records. UAF Alaska and Polar Regions Collections holds photographs and oral histories from the region. The Northwest Arctic Borough School District historical records document community life. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Northwest Arctic area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Petersburg Borough created 2013 from the former Wrangell-Petersburg Census Area. Founded 1897 by Norwegian immigrant Peter Buschmann who established a cannery and sawmill. Strong Norwegian heritage reflected in the Little Norway Festival. Fishing (halibut, salmon, shrimp, crab) is the primary industry. One of Alaska's newest boroughs.
county_metadata.research_tips: Petersburg Borough was created in 2013 from a portion of the former Wrangell-Petersburg Census Area. For records before 2013, search under Wrangell-Petersburg. Petersburg was founded in 1897 by Norwegian immigrant Peter Buschmann, and Norwegian heritage is central to the community. Lutheran church records (Petersburg Lutheran Church, est. 1900) are at the congregation and the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. The Clausen Memorial Museum in Petersburg holds local family papers, fishing industry records, and photographs documenting the Norwegian fishing community. Immigration and naturalization records for Norwegian settlers are at NARA Pacific Alaska Region. Federal census from 1900 for Petersburg. Tlingit peoples (Kake and surrounding areas) have records at BIA NARA and Sealaska Heritage Institute. BLM GLO patents searchable online. Petersburg city records at the Petersburg Borough Clerk's office.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Petersburg area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.parent_county: Prince of Wales-Outer Ketchikan Census Area (reorganized)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Prince of Wales-Hyder Census Area reorganized 2008 from Prince of Wales-Outer Ketchikan Census Area. Covers Prince of Wales Island (third largest island in the U.S.) and the mainland community of Hyder on the Canadian border. Home to Tlingit and Haida peoples. Timber, fishing, and mining have been the primary industries. Craig, Klawock, and Hydaburg are the major communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Prince of Wales-Hyder Census Area covers Prince of Wales Island and Hyder in Southeast Alaska. Tlingit and Haida peoples are central to the area's history -- clan records at Sealaska Heritage Institute in Juneau and the Haida Heritage Foundation in Hydaburg. BIA records at NARA include school records for the Hydaburg Industrial School and census rolls. Russian Orthodox records for older communities at the Library of Congress (microfilm). Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. Timber and mining records from Prince of Wales Island operations are at UAF and BLM Alaska. Federal census from 1880 for Southeast Alaska. The area was reorganized in 2008 from Prince of Wales-Outer Ketchikan Census Area; for earlier records search under the former designation. CCC-era (Civilian Conservation Corps) records from Prince of Wales Island at NARA. BLM GLO patents searchable online. Community records may be at village tribal offices in Craig, Klawock, and Hydaburg.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Prince of Wales-Hyder area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.parent_county: Unified City and Borough of Sitka formed from Greater Sitka Borough
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sitka City and Borough formed 1971. Former capital of Russian America (New Archangel, 1799-1867) and capital of the District/Territory of Alaska (1867-1906). Site of the 1804 Battle of Sitka between Tlingit and Russian forces. St. Michael's Cathedral (1848, rebuilt after 1966 fire) is a landmark of Russian Orthodox heritage. Sheldon Jackson School (1878-2007) educated Alaska Native students from across the territory.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sitka City and Borough was the capital of Russian America and is one of Alaska's most historically significant communities. Russian colonial records from 1799 (founding of New Archangel/Sitka) are the richest in Alaska -- the Sitka Parish of St. Michael's Cathedral has records from the early 1800s at the Library of Congress (microfilm) and the Orthodox Church in America archives. The Sitka Historical Society and Sheldon Jackson Museum hold colonial-era artifacts, documents, and Tlingit cultural materials. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court/probate files. Tlingit (Sheet'ka Kwaan) records at Sealaska Heritage Institute. The Sitka National Historical Park archives hold Russian-Tlingit contact records. Sheldon Jackson School records (1878-2007) document Alaska Native students from across the territory -- at the Presbyterian Historical Society and Sitka. Federal census from 1880 for Sitka. Customs house records (Sitka was the customs port of entry) at NARA. BLM GLO patents searchable online.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Sitka area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

Essential for tracing Creole, Alaska Native, and Russian families in the colonial and early American periods

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Skagway Municipality (formerly Borough, organized 2007 from Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area). Gateway to the Klondike Gold Rush (1897-1900) via the White Pass. The White Pass and Yukon Route Railway (1898) connected Skagway to the Yukon. Notorious for con man Soapy Smith. Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park preserves the historic downtown. Population peaked at about 10,000 during the gold rush.
county_metadata.research_tips: Skagway Municipality is the gateway to the Klondike Gold Rush (1897-1900) and one of Alaska's most historically documented small communities. The Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park archives (NPS) hold extensive records including passenger manifests, business records, photographs, and oral histories of gold rush families. Alaska State Archives has territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. The Skagway Museum has local family papers, town records, and White Pass and Yukon Route Railway employee records. Canadian border crossing records for Chilkoot Pass and White Pass are at Library and Archives Canada and NARA. Federal census from 1900 for Skagway. Tlingit (Chilkoot) family records at Sealaska Heritage Institute. Cemetery records at the Gold Rush Cemetery are well-documented. Skagway was part of Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area until 2007; for census data use that designation for pre-2007 records. Skagway newspapers (Daily Alaskan from 1898) at Alaska State Library and Library of Congress.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Skagway area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Southeast Fairbanks Census Area covers the upper Tanana Valley in interior Alaska. Unorganized census area. Communities include Tok (highway junction), Delta Junction, and Eagle. The Alaska Highway (1942) passes through the area. Eagle was an early judicial center (court records from 1900). Fortymile gold rush (1886) predates the more famous Klondike rush. Fort Greely (est. 1942) near Delta Junction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Southeast Fairbanks Census Area covers the upper Tanana Valley including Tok, Delta Junction, and Eagle. The community of Eagle on the Yukon River was an early judicial seat (Third District, later Fourth District) with court records from 1900 at Alaska State Archives. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Fourth Judicial District court records. Fort Greely military records (Cold War era, est. 1942) are at NARA St. Louis. BIA records at NARA include census rolls and school records for Upper Tanana Athabascan communities. The Alaska Highway construction records (1942) at NARA document workers and communities along the route through the area. Federal census from 1900 for Eagle and the upper Tanana. Gold mining records from the Fortymile district (1886, pre-Klondike gold rush) are at BLM Alaska and UAF. The Eagle Historical Society and BLM Eagle office hold local records. BLM GLO patents searchable online. Delta Junction area homesteading records (1940s-1960s) at BLM Alaska.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Southeast Fairbanks area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Southeast Fairbanks Bureau of Indian Affairs Records -- NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region (Anchorage/Seattle)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Alaska Native communities in Southeast Fairbanks area including census rolls, school enrollment records, allotment files, and agency correspondence. Also includes ANCSA (Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, 1971) enrollment records at the BIA Alaska Regional Office. Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines

source_profiles.source_name: Southeast Fairbanks Land Patents, Homesteads, and Mining Claims -- BLM GLO / Alaska DNR
source_profiles.repository: BLM General Land Office Records (online) / Alaska DNR Recorder's Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents and homestead entries for Southeast Fairbanks area searchable on BLM GLO Records website. Alaska DNR Recorder's Office holds recorded documents (deeds, mortgages) for the recording district covering Southeast Fairbanks. Mining claims (both lode and placer) at BLM Alaska State Office. Alaska was a public land state -- most land was federal domain

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mining records for Southeast Fairbanks area including placer and lode claims, company records, and employment files at BLM Alaska, UAF, and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: Southeast Fairbanks Military Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA St. Louis / NARA Pacific Alaska Region
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military installation records for Southeast Fairbanks area at NARA St. Louis and NARA Pacific Alaska Region

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Wrangell City and Borough formed 2008 from the former Wrangell-Petersburg Census Area. Has been governed by four nations: Tlingit, Russia (1834 stockade), Britain (1840 HBC lease), and the U.S. (1867). One of the oldest non-Native settlements in Alaska. Fort Wrangell was an important military and trading post. Gateway to the Stikine River route during gold rush eras. Fishing and timber are traditional industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wrangell City and Borough has Tlingit, Russian, British, and American heritage layers. The oldest non-Native settlement records date from the Russian stockade (Redoubt Saint Dionysius, 1834) and the British Hudson's Bay Company lease period (1840-1849). Russian Orthodox records for Wrangell at the Library of Congress. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. The Wrangell Museum holds local family papers, photographs, and records from the fishing, timber, and mining industries. Tlingit (Shtax'heen Kwaan) records at Sealaska Heritage Institute. Fort Wrangell military records (1868-1877) at NARA document the early American period. BIA records at NARA include school records for Wrangell Institute (1932-1975). Federal census from 1880 for Wrangell. Wrangell was part of Wrangell-Petersburg Census Area until 2008; for earlier records use that designation. Gold rush outfitting records (Stikine River route to Cassiar and Klondike) at the Wrangell Museum. BLM GLO patents searchable online.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Wrangell area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

source_profiles.source_name: Wrangell Territorial and State Vital Records -- Alaska State Archives / Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.repository: Alaska State Archives, Juneau / Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial birth and death registrations for Wrangell area from 1913; marriage records from territorial period; modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Pre-1913 vital events must be traced through church records, Russian Orthodox parish registers, and missionary records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Territorial records generally complete from 1913; earlier coverage varies greatly by community

source_profiles.source_name: Wrangell Probate and Court Records -- First Judicial District Superior Court
source_profiles.repository: Alaska Superior Court, First Judicial District / Alaska State Archives, Juneau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court handles probate (wills, estates, guardianships), adoption, and civil cases for Wrangell. First Judicial District records. Older territorial court files transferred to Alaska State Archives. Commissioner's court records from the territorial period may predate borough organization

county_metadata.parent_county: Unorganized territory (First Judicial District, Yakutat portion)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Yakutat City and Borough organized 1992. Remote community on the Gulf of Alaska coast between Southeast and Southcentral Alaska. Home to Yakutat Tlingit peoples. Swedish Free Mission (later Covenant Church) established a mission in the 1880s. Isolated by road -- accessible by air and sea. Known for surfing and Hubbard Glacier. Yakutat was a military installation during WWII.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yakutat City and Borough covers a remote stretch of the Gulf of Alaska coast where Tlingit (Yakutat Kwaan) peoples have lived for centuries. Swedish Free Mission (later Evangelical Covenant Church) records from the 1880s are at the Covenant Archives and Historical Library in Chicago. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and First Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include census rolls and school records for Yakutat. The Yakutat Tlingit Tribe cultural center holds oral histories and family records. Commercial cannery records (Yakutat and Southern Railway, Libby cannery) from the early 1900s at UAF and company archives. Federal census from 1880 for Yakutat. The 1899 Harriman Alaska Expedition documented Yakutat -- records at the Smithsonian Archives. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska. Yakutat organized as a City and Borough in 1992; for earlier records check First Judicial District court files in Juneau.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Yakutat area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Yukon-Koyukuk Census Area is the largest census area by land area in Alaska and the U.S. Covers the central Yukon and Koyukuk River drainages. Unorganized census area. Predominantly Koyukon and Gwich'in Athabascan population. Fort Yukon (est. 1847 by Hudson's Bay Company) is the oldest English-speaking settlement in Alaska. Gold mining on the Koyukuk River from the 1890s. Galena is the unofficial administrative center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yukon-Koyukuk Census Area is Alaska's largest by area, covering the central Yukon River and Koyukuk River regions. Koyukon, Gwich'in, and other Athabascan peoples are the predominant population. Episcopal Church mission records from St. John's-in-the-Wilderness (Allakaket, 1906) and other Yukon River missions are at the Diocese of Alaska archives. Catholic mission records from Holy Cross Mission (1888) and Nulato Mission (1887) are at Gonzaga University. Alaska State Archives holds territorial vital records from 1913 and Fourth Judicial District court records. BIA records at NARA include census rolls, school records, and allotment files for villages along the Yukon and Koyukuk Rivers. Federal census from 1880 for the Yukon River region but very sparse before 1910. Gold mining records from the Koyukuk district and Yukon River communities at BLM Alaska and UAF. Hudson's Bay Company records (Ft. Yukon, 1847-1869) at Hudson's Bay Company Archives in Winnipeg. BLM GLO patents and Alaska Native allotment records at BLM Alaska. Oral history collections at UAF are especially valuable for this region.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO Records online -- federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Yukon-Koyukuk area; Alaska DNR Recorder's Office for recorded documents
